The 7 Sisters

Written in 2022

7 Sisters video: [https://vimeo.com/704688254](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://vimeo.com/704688254&sa=D&source=editors&ust=1777400035441019&usg=AOvVaw0QUWpU6RJp6gXhX6pAYqA8)

Tom Busby has developed the DTI Method over the years to help our students learn a repeatable process to analyze and trade the markets. A process must have a starting point. For DTI and students of the DTI Method, every trading year, month, week, day and even down to intraday time frames starts with the 7 Sisters. This white paper will first define the 7 Sisters, introduce the basics of each market and briefly describe the purpose of tracking the global markets.

7 Sisters:

ES - S&P futures

YM - Dow futures

NQ - Nasdaq futures

DAX - German index

CL - crude futures

GC - gold futures

ZB - 30 year bond futures

*   ES - E-mini S&P futures. A derivative S&P 500 cash index. The actual futures contract is trading at the CME electronically on a computer system called Globex. Trading for the week begins Sunday night at 5pm Central time and closes for the week Friday at 4pm Central. Each trading day is separated by a 1 hour halt in trading from 4pm to 5pm Central time
*   NQ - E-mini Nasdaq futures. Based on the Nasdaq 100 index and not the Composite index. On the equity this is similar to the QQQ which is based on the Nasdaq 100 and not the Composite index. Also traded at the CME on Globex and has the same trading hours as ES.
*   YM - E-mini Dow futures. Based on the Dow 30, also known as the Dow Jones Industrial Average. Also traded at the CME on Globex and has the same trading hours as ES and NQ.
*   DAX - Mini DAX futures from Germany. Just like the U.S. Dow index as it consists of 30 of the largest companies in Germany. Traded on the EUREX (European Exchange) electronically and its currency is in Euros. Trading hours are 6:15pm Central to 3pm Central the next day, opening the week’s trading Sunday night and closing out the week Friday afternoon.
*   GC - Gold futures. Traded at the CME on Globex and has the same trading hours as the ES, NQ and YM, opening the week Sunday night at 5pm Central and closing the week Friday afternoon at 4pm Central, with a one hour break in between each trading day. This is a commodity futures market.
*   CL - Crude oil futures. Also traded at the CME on Globex and also has the same trading hours as the ES, NQ, YM and GC. Like gold, the crude futures contract is a commodity futures market, and it is deliverable.
*   ZB - 30 year bond futures. Trade at the CME on Globex as well, with the same trading hours as our other Globex markets. This contract controls a $100,000 U.S. Treasury Bond.

What we are really looking for when tracking the 7 Sisters is we have the indexes, the ES, YM and NQ futures (if you would prefer to track the respective ETF’s during cash market hours that would be the SPY, DIA and QQQ), and we get to see if our indexes are moving higher or lower. If the indexes are moving lower you will approximately 70-75% of all stocks moving lower. If the indexes are moving higher you should expect around 70-75% of stocks moving higher.

When we look at the DAX we of course want to see how Europe is doing. Germany is the largest economy in Europe so we get to see how the largest economy in Europe is doing. The ETF for the DAX is not a heavily traded market so it is not a great replacement. Instead you can track a calculated price of the DAX here: https://www.investing.com/indices/germany-30-futures and the feed is very nearly a live feed.

GLD is the ETF equivalent that does a decent job tracking gold futures. Gold is a fear contract or it can be viewed as an inflation hedge as well. If gold is moving higher there is a lot of uncertainty in the market. Of course you will have currencies moving the gold market also. For example, if the U.S. Dollar is getting weak and we will see money flow into gold.

USO is our ETF for crude. The question you should ask yourself is are we having to pay higher energy prices or are energy prices lower? Energy affects everything in the U.S. and around the world so we watch energy prices.

TLT is our preferred ETF for the bond market. Bond prices are of course sensitive to interest rates, inversely. As interest rates rise, bond prices fall. As interest rates fall, bond prices rise. TLT is based off the 20 year bond instead of the 30 year bond so it is a slightly different look than the 30 year bond futures. Of course, changes in interest can have significant impacts on the health of the economy, so we must track them.

For more specifics about each of our 7 Sisters markets, please consider reviewing Geof Smith’s video introduction here: [https://vimeo.com/704688254](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://vimeo.com/704688254&sa=D&source=editors&ust=1777400035447637&usg=AOvVaw0LMyQdm16JxE1Y2V0nkP2V)

Orientation to DTI’s Reference Bars

Written in 2020

Our goal is to analyze the 24-hour market and to answer the question, long, short or out. We do so by breaking up the global trading day into four time segments: Asia, Europe, US morning and US afternoon. Reference the picture below (in Central Time).

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg23CXCg_B95LuIz-mhuVS0G6wxQLalXOz2KUkCj2MQtb_2pwYS_ixNfRAW2ABVYqAX1VvOkf79lz70X7Xv9F82IyNqYXAMVQgXUAtkwMa2kNc41VygV9TcomPdZ1yMWkHXgII8yVGX83VlVPsa0AJ6TPFRcDfsQiQ=s2048)

To keep everything in perspective, we track the 24-hour global market using our proprietary approach with DTI Reference Bars. A reference bar is simply a 30-minute candlestick or bar that appears colored in blue on our charts inside the RoadMap™ Software. On many occasions you will hear Tom mention the reference bars as “blue bars” … and in total, we use 4 blue bars to analyze and trade the 24- hour market. I’ve listed them below.

*   Reference Bar 1 (RB1), occurring from 5:00 pm CT – 5:30 pm CT is used to analyze and trade Asia’s market
*   Reference Bar 2 (RB2), occurring from 3:30 am CT – 4:00 am CT is used to analyze and trade Europe’s market
*   Reference Bar 3 (RB3), occurring from 8:30 am CT – 9:00 am CT is used to analyze and trade the U.S. morning market hours
*   Reference Bar 4 (RB4), occurring from 12:30 pm CT – 1:00 pm CT is used to analyze and trade the U.S. afternoon market hours

Simple Strategy for Short-term Trading

As another guideline for learning the DTI Method and the use of Reference Bars when trading during the day, I think it is fitting to go over the 3 main chart patterns you need to know to make money trading. These patterns generally tell you when to be long, short or stay out. Here’s a picture to display these simple but very effective chart patterns.

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg2r4avpOLQthhe3qlrxci1n_NjX8rnytADvnE_4W7MkCebI3xKm6Tc0Ra8_CXqDiYhdH6EYjqfxTgR-BosheMBn1kl7zjgZcRqI_rlFXhEHtDrcw8DqUh6ykLFtuBGEvKq3e4cylAcwj8KdfZ3toHb8C5nbfqfPXvFc=s2048)

Now let’s incorporate the Reference Bars with these 3 signals. Each Reference Bar maps out a 30-minute range. Note the high and low of that range. Using the simple patterns from above, we have a buy signal when the range of the Reference Bar is broken to the upside. If this occurs we then answer the question of where our stop goes. A simple strategy to use is if you enter a long trade off a higher high of the Reference Bar, your stop goes below the low of that same Reference Bar, as shown below

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg01Jah54KCYYUEZbTSdmBWXJ5JyZcpbAk93COFCap3TYjnLIyekhqMzk2-ys0QZievfEKr-aTeZJ8r0PP7RFL3vKsBQihMocy2dnGy8VWWcSDKJiOTcU0Qk-udPcrlUi6Lr22cwuWMIlTfo7zDumG5Ap4MrbY7R_Q=s2048)

Please do not get caught up in exact numbers. You don’t have to enter the trade one tick above the RB range; all the same as you don’t need to sell out on a stop one tick below the RB range. There is nothing exact in trading. Our goal is to catch trends intraday, and the Reference Bars set us up to capture those trends.

Let’s look at a chart for more clarity. On the chart below of the Emini S&P futures, you will see 30 minute bars, with a few of them colored blue. The blue bars are the Reference Bars. Technically, those are candlesticks and not bars, but the candlesticks are easier to see. For the DTI Method, either will work. Our Reference Bar times occur at the beginning of each Time Segment. They are 5:00pm CT, 3:30am CT, 8:30am CT and 12:30pm CT. A break in either direction of the Reference Bar range is usually an indication that traders want the take the market in that direction. If the signal reverses, and then the other side of the Reference Bar is taken out, that is a strong reversal of trend signal.

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg2BA8YUmAHvc6aXE3Zkx-z-mEZuMR-UUilD5peyOJl4wbsc1VmM2HwJA8vC5biBmgtfZZ8GZlv8rI45KQxZegQ2J1lzbn-CQU1mvf2crqcexcAN1UHNv12qqlsOiPRCMgnML1iVwSKMTLu-Fnb7nHjlMl5O-CfoaA=s2048)

You can’t tell, but the candle at the far left is a 12:30pm Reference Bar. It signifies the beginning of our 4th Time Segment during the 24-hour market. I’ve labeled it RB4, and proceeded to label all other Reference Bars on this chart. At the beginning of this chart, sellers took control during the afternoon hours of the U.S. market. Once the futures market opens up again (the first RB1), sellers remain in control and the downtrend continues. Once Europe opens (RB2), more buyers step in, reversing the trend. At the U.S. cash open at 8:30am CT (RB3), we again get a reversal, this time the selling pressure from the end of the prior day regains focus. We then have yet another reversal when traders return from lunch (RB4) and finish the trading day with a rally. A very similar pattern occurs the next day.

These reversals are unmistakable, and our placement of Reference Bar times is no coincidence. The RB4 reversal is one of the strongest trades you will find, and our proprietary Reference Bars make this crystal clear. You won’t find charts like this anywhere else but DTI’s RoadMap™ Software.

\-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Addition of RB X, Y and Z – White Reference Bars

In recent years we have added to our key Reference Bar times throughout the day. These additional times can be found below:

\-          Reference Bar X (RBX), occurring from 6:00 am CT – 6:30 am CT is used to analyze and trade the U.S. pre-market and Europe after lunch

\-          Reference Bar Y (RBY - Genesis), occurring from 10:30 am CT – 11:00 am CT is used to analyze and trade following the European close

\-          Reference Bar Z (RBZ), occurring from 2:30 pm CT – 3:00 pm CT is used to analyze and trade into the close for the U.S. market

Note that these 3 additional RBs are colored white on our RoadMap™ charts. The example below shows the S&P making a low and reversal back higher going into and after the European close RBY. Remember that each Reference Bar brings about an opportunity for the trend to accelerate or reverse. We call the RBY bar “Genesis” as it marks the first time throughout the entire trading day that the U.S. takes full control over the markets, and on many days a new trend can begin at the RBY time once any influence from Europe drops off.

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg20QdzerqXuurtqReflRNK7bzt2sxq-wcj6-D-M7YuqzEAiJ1e2YQXf4pkF4NL-mTFc7q4XrqwQ2WRJZwNd5d4SEWLNMB-n0E4RJnTKyVpjo4QCpoY4CRU9GVZ0IHqgEgK_aLYyZa6X58onKtZfVdKKdzzAScyJ8x-rmg=s2048)

\-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Now let’s go a step further with a concept from Tom Busby that allows him to get a jump start on finding the trend:

“What I like to do is take the high and low (range) of the current Reference Bar (RB) and find the midpoint of that range. I then compare the currently traded price to that midpoint to determine if the immediate trend is stronger or weaker. I’ll continue to compare whatever the currently traded price is to that midpoint for the entire Time Segment, until the next Reference Bar forms, and then I’ll start the process over again.”

“As an example, if the ES opened up the cash market trading at 8:30am CT and then mapped out a 10- point range from 3350-3360 during the first 30 minutes of trading, the midpoint would be 3355. After the RB forms, as long as we are above the 3355 midpoint we are more bullish than bearish, and vice versa. “Again the midpoint of each Reference Bar is important to tell us whether we are stronger or weaker, and because we know that trends can change around the key times of day of our RB’s, we can use the midpoint to get an early indication of what kind of day (or Time Segment) we are going to have. Example: if we are bullish coming into the afternoon RB4 and the trend looks to be continuing after RB4 forms… then all of a sudden price reverses and falls below the midpoint of RB4, that’s an early indication that something is wrong.”

“Now, obviously if it breaks the low of the RB it’s an even better trade to the downside, and the same is true for the opposite scenario. This is just a real simple technique I’ve been using for years and we’re including it in the RB white paper so that everyone has a chance to understand entries and exits on different markets, and it works 24 hours around the clock. I observe it all the time in my head and it just occurred to me that I’ve never shared this knowledge in all the years I’ve been teaching.”

\-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

That’s it. Learn DTI’s Reference Bar and learn Tom’s method, the DTI Method and you will be a much more accurate trader.

Pivots the TBUZ Way

Written in 2017

Trading futures can be exciting and profitable, especially for seasoned veterans. Futures can also be quite intimidating when you first start to trade them, even for the most experienced of stock traders. Prices move fast and at first they may seemingly move randomly. On some occasions they move in the opposite direction of what makes sense. The truth is you will never be fully prepared for every dip and rally in the market. What you can do is develop a process that keeps you on the winning side making money and gets you out when the market trades against you.

Numerous different indicators are touted as the key to trading. Indicators such as moving averages, momentum oscillators, and countless others can work and work well in certain situations. However there is only one approach to trading the market that Tom Busby uses throughout the year to successfully navigate any time frame. I am talking about pivot numbers. Pivot numbers have held the test of time. They make sense, are tradeable, and can be used to indicate market direction during any time frame.

What is a pivot number?

In the simplest terms, a pivot number is a price level that past trading history indicates as important. Pivot numbers can be mathematically calculated price levels and are often times used by floor traders as predictive indicators of future price movements. The standard calculation of your main pivot number for the day is a simple average of the prior day’s high, low and close ((H + L + C) / 3). Further pivot numbers are then calculated off the main pivot number. These levels are very important and should be respected. The reason they work is because the big money floor traders use them.

You will also find market generated pivot numbers by just watching the tape. These are support and resistance levels which naturally form throughout the day as the price movement stalls at a certain level and “teeter-totters” back and forth. All pivot numbers act as support and resistance levels and the same sentiment rule applies for each. If price breaks above a pivot, sentiment is more bullish. If price breaks below a pivot, sentiment is more bearish.

There are many different interpretations for how to calculate pivot numbers, but like most indicators, they are not perfect and depend on the time frame in which you are trading. Time is a central factor within the DTI Method. For the remainder of this article I will walk you through how to use the pivot numbers setup for you through the RoadMap™ Software. I will also explain Tom Busby’s time-based pivot numbers that he has realized hold great importance throughout the year.

Compass Chart

Tom Busby and Geof Smith developed the RoadMap™ Software to guide you through the four time segments of the 24-hour global market. These four time segments include Asia (17:00CT – 03:30CT), Europe (03:30CT – 08:30CT), the US morning (08:30CT – 12:30CT) and the US afternoon (12:30CT – 16:00). DTI’s Compass Chart provides for you the time-based pivot numbers to guide you through each time segment. Take a look at the following compass chart. This is an ideal chart to show you how DTI’s Buy Zone and Sell Zone pivot numbers can act as both support and resistance throughout each time segment.

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg3kMaR04uNP1tZ-VE1SAL30TunXRI1_lgR_xrgpz_8_14BiIDIlmApYYq-6Xun_9z3pkrYYjcysHsvaszUAGssK4XuA96eW1ofV5eLFKDAoBVSMXGjCfDtyUqFtN6EvmXgB7aEIJoN8xGPx66VtGDdkIEPoukfIiw=s2048)

Notice how the low of the time frame shown occurred during the 08:30CT reference bar. The sell zone pivot acted as support and the buy zone pivot acted as resistance during the first 30 minutes of this time segment. You will notice the buy and sell zones often times act as resistance and support respectively until a breakout occurs. The reference bar open (initial midpoint) and buy zone pivot would have been logical profit targets if a long position was taken off the support held at the sell zone pivot. Soon after 09:00CT a true breakout trade occurred. The move in the ES was strong enough to not only trade right through the Buy Zone Target but also right through the previous all-time high of 2480.50, another pivot number you should follow closely.

The spike up in prices was a normal move as buy stop orders were triggered once the new high was broken. Also a normal trading move is the profit taking which occurred following the move up in prices. This is commonly called “reversion” and notice how the ES tracked right back down to the important pivot numbers. In most instances the pivot numbers displayed on the Compass Chart will hold precedence. Remember though that an all-time high will always hold more importance if the two pivot numbers fall close together.

Time-based Pivot Numbers

Yearly Opens

Every new trading year brings exciting new opportunities. Sometimes a popular trading strategy like the Dogs of Dow works wonderfully. Other times the lagging sectors and industries from the years prior will rebound, offering great investments. Recovering economic conditions can sometimes lead to small-cap stocks outperforming large-cap stocks. Regardless of the broad strategy or strategies you enjoy following, remember this: none of these strategies work 100% of the time and you must have a method to deciding when to get out if the trade goes south.

Here at DTI we pay more attention to opens rather than closes. The open for any time frame displays trader sentiment from the moment trading begins for that period. The close of the prior period may act as important support or resistance, but does not accurately display trader sentiment. Record the opening of the year prices for the markets you are trading. If above the yearly open, you should look to go long. If below the yearly open, you should look to get short or stay out of the market. The yearly open should be used as a pivot number for your trades you plan to hold for 6 months or longer.

Quarterly Opens

The 1st of April, July, and October are key levels and holding these levels will be very important to keep the trend positive. This strategy will keep you from trying to pick a bottom. The same long, short or out rule used for the yearly open applies here. Use the quarterly open for trades you plan to hold for 1-6 months.

Monthly and Weekly Opens

DTI has developed a new trading strategy and educational series titled “Weekly Options.” Monthly and weekly opens are very important numbers to trade this strategy and both should be used in tandem with the other. If the stock you are trading is above the monthly open you should look to go long. However before you blindly go long for the week you should first gauge where weekly prices will be heading. Tom prefers to use the weekly open and the 9:30am CT open on Tuesday as his key numbers for the week. If the 9:30 open is above the weekly open, and the weekly open is above the monthly open, go long. If the stock turns around and trades below the weekly open, you need to look to get out of your position. Taking this approach will greatly increase your accuracy.

A pivot number is a point in the market that tells you whether to be long or short. Tom uses these pivot numbers to help him decide how and where to take risk. Every morning in his TBUZ TV daily market update, Tom gives his key pivot numbers for the day. Be sure to sign up for Tom’s daily market update, TBUZ TV, by going to [https://www.dtitrader.com/abouttbuztv/](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://www.dtitrader.com/abouttbuztv/&sa=D&source=editors&ust=1777400035478031&usg=AOvVaw1KbBKbMcHVHCXY_vtxkUpt)

Below is a daily chart of Netflix (NFLX). Netflix opened the month of August on a Tuesday at $182.49 (first blue arrow). The following Monday August 7th Netflix opened the week at $181 (second blue arrow), lower than the monthly open. This was our first clue that we may look to go short Netflix for the week. On Tuesday (red arrow) the 9:30am CT open was $179.95 which was lower than the weekly open. This was our confirmation to open a weekly short position. Netflix closed the week at $171.40 for a very profitable short trade.

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg1cRvJyz_MfxeueOUxw_Uxme8j61e2J6m1M2aWt6WfFXdyX0cUqRM6CqbUen6H5DjUuMfkv0Z3Qtv-v_Zoh_QgEXVSF7l3SgllMbkT1hdJZasbczSl3qmwyWpBQ26J6SzjVyTcs83-5DfoENQGA66gDyB202VmAIW9C=s2048)

Midpoint

A simple pivot number is the midpoint of any time frame. If above the midpoint the market is bullish. If below the midpoint the market is bearish. A simple mathematical average calculated between the high and low of the period is all you need. The RoadMap™ Software will do this for you within the Research Key. Tom also likes to add the midpoint to his Custom Page. To add a midpoint column to your custom page, right click on the custom page and select “insert column.” Next, right click the custom page again, select “Column Heading” and type in “Mid.” Always look for the opening price around that same time frame. The open will hold more importance than the midpoint if the two are close to the same price level.

Reference Bars

The first 30 minutes of each time segment form the blue reference bars on the compass chart. The opens of each reference bar are very important pivot numbers for intraday trading. You will notice that the reference bar open functions as the midpoint between the buy and sell zones. If above the reference bar open we should be watching our stock or futures contract with anticipation of going long once the compass chart gives a buy signal within the buy zone. If below the reference bar open we should be waiting to go short once we get a sell signal. This does not mean to go long or short at any time in which prices are above or below the reference bar open. Doing so will usually get yourself caught in a reversal such as the example below. Realize that this can be an issue in your trading during the overnight market. The overnight market is typically very slow which may encourage you to trade out of boredom at times. Don’t do this!

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg15tzzRAdNLMTDBk-wj_HrBYov7HtiumHF_CaBz1Kwnf8JKPclRnv5Zg3F7kPPGJSbxLreSpQPMMq4tsm1C0McO9kXQMD3I5BQ6LSQHi5A9i4UcIHXyftWynuGobU0LeyVOZl7RCjWZ5tO0tvWpC1Rd_k5hnefukQ=s2048)

To start the segment the ES was trading below the open but never entered the sell zone. Shorting the ES below the reference bar open without a confirmation would have resulted in losses due to a reversal. A buy limit could have been placed at the sell zone pivot to go long. The ES then traded back above the open, turning the intraday sentiment bullish, and eventually confirmed a long breakout position. Notice how the weekly open was below the monthly open. Even though these long trades could have worked, the odds were not in your favor. The sentiment for the month was bearish. A strong argument could have been made to not take either of these two long trades.

Remember that markets have a tendency to trade toward key pivot numbers. For instance, it should not surprise you for the market to retest weekly opens at any time throughout the week. Time-based pivot numbers such as yearly, quarterly, monthly, and weekly opens, as well as alltime highs or lows will hold great importance in determining market sentiment. Learn these key pivot numbers and test them in your trading. You will find them to be useful tools to help you become a more profitable trader.

Test Yourself

Q: A pivot number will indicate whether you should look to go long or short?

A: True. If prices are above a pivot number the sentiment is bullish. If prices are below a pivot number the sentiment is bearish.

Q: What price level should you find for every time frame you trade?

A: The open is the key indicator for market sentiment during any time segment.

Q: What are the two numbers to use for DTI’s Weekly Options trades?

A: The open of the week and the 09:30CT open on Tuesday.

Key Numbers to Establish for Trading Success

Written in 2021

While the purpose of this white paper is simple and straight-forward, it is perhaps the most important white paper for you to thoroughly read through and understand. When trading the markets, a mis click of the mouse can cost you money. Likewise, a misinterpretation of directional advice Tom gives can also cost you money. Therefore, we hope you take the necessary time to digest the thoughts below and put them into practice. First, we will identify the four key numbers / concepts defined in this white paper. Then below you will find a deep dive from Tom into how they work.

Tom Busby’s Top 4 Key Numbers to Establish for Trading Success:

Trigger

All Bets Off

Target

Pivot

Let’s dig in.

Folks, there are certain structures setup in successful trading. I have found that you need three main numbers for every trade you enter, then a fourth key number to help keep you on the winning side of the market.

The first key number with every trade is the “Trigger” number. I define the Trigger number as a price the market must go to for me to take action, or basically make the trade. For example, if a stock I think is setting up for a bullish move is currently trading at 140.50, I might set a Trigger number at 141. That means only if the stock goes above 141 will I take the trade. If it doesn’t go above 141 then I don’t take the trade. Many new traders get confused with this concept. They think that if you are willing to go long at 141 then why not go long at 140.50? I’ve found that this is the wrong way to think and approach the markets, is a rash and impatient way to play the markets, and often leads to bad trades. Simply put, my Trigger number forces the stock to prove that I need to be long. If it doesn’t prove itself then it doesn’t earn a spot in my account.

The second number is the “All Bets Off” number. The All Bets Off (ABO) number for me means that if a trade sets up to go in a certain direction, but then goes in the opposite direction there is a number in which this trade is no longer good. Essentially my ABO number is a stop loss, but it also functions as a signal to never enter a trade. Let’s establish a Trigger and All Bets Off number as an example and go over two scenarios. Assume a stock I’m watching is trading at 140.5 and I set the Trigger number at 141 and All Bets Off number at 139. Scenario 1: if the stock goes above 141 I enter the trade. If the stock goes up then great, I can take my profits and move to the next trade. However, if the stock reverses after I enter and goes below my ABO number of 139, then I know my analysis is wrong and I need to stop myself out of the trade. Scenario 2: the stock does not go above 141 Trigger and instead goes below 139 ABO. This is my clue that my initial analysis was wrong and I should forget about this trade altogether. Lucky for me I used a process and got a warning sign before even entering the trade, thus saving my account from experiencing a loss. These two scenarios showcase how the Trigger and All Bets Off work in unison for every trade.

Another layer to the trading process would be setting a Target. My target is usually a short-term target and this can also cause some confusion for new traders. Let me explain. I’ll set an initial short-term target where I will take action and take profits, but there is no reason to think that a good trade will stop going up just because it hits my initial target. During certain market moves it makes sense to take profit on half of my position or a third of my position, and then set a stop loss on the remainder to see how far the trade will run. I always set an initial target, though sometimes I might even revise my target, but I will always take action if my initial target is hit by making half of my position off, or maybe sometimes take full profit and move on.

Now what if you get a trade that is past the trigger and close to target? Well, I don’t go after the trade if it’s above the midpoint between the trigger and target numbers. For example, let’s say my trigger is 145 and my target is 147. If the stock is above 146, halfway to the target point, then I might not take the trade until it pulls back some to test my trigger number, or I might just pass on the trade entirely. An important note though is if you do buy on a pullback down to trigger, the All Bets Off number still stands and if taken out the trade should be exited.

So, there can be some finesse in entering sometimes if you are not actively watching the markets and taking action accordingly, but the importance of the three main numbers holds true. Again the three numbers are the Trigger (where I’m going to enter the trade), the All Bets Off (if I’m in the trade where to bail, or if I’m not in where to forget about the trade and just move on) and the Target (where I’m right about market direction and will take profits). I have those three numbers identified before I ever take a trade.

There is one other term that is important to my trading and that is Pivot. The Pivot number tells me that the market is overall bullish (above pivot) or overall bearish (below pivot) for a defined time frame. For example, let’s say I set a Weekly Pivot number on the S&P 500 futures at 4320. If the S&P is above 4320 then it is bullish on the week. If the S&P is below 4320 then it is bearish for the week. Sometimes I’ll even look longer term and use a Monthly Pivot. At times the market will also have a big move and the Weekly Pivot may not seem relevant anymore. In those instances I’ll establish a Daily Pivot to make short-term decisions on. The Daily Pivot is good for stop management and I’ll typically first look at the weekly or monthly midpoint to establish the Daily Pivot. For example, if I am long a stock at 140, the stock is making new highs for the week and/or month and the midpoint continues to rise, then I’ll raise my stop loss to somewhere relatively close below that midpoint, as I let the stock I’m in ideally go higher and higher and higher.

That’s the way I trade. There are probably other ways to trade that may be better, but this process sure works for me. Pivot, Trigger, Target and All Bets Off are the numbers you need to have a handle on when you’re trading the markets.

I have one last insight for you: always use the underlying stock price to establish these key numbers, whether you are trading stock, options or option spreads. With options and spreads, it is hard to set these numbers on the option price because of liquidity issues. That’s a topic for another time, but take it from a seasoned veteran and always use the underlying stock price when setting key numbers.

Setting up the Week  
Written in 2021

Every Sunday night Tom holds his weekly planning session at 8pm CT. He takes this time to educate and inform students on how to go about mapping out the trading week. The first step is to find the key levels on the ES. If you are not familiar with the ES, it is the E-mini futures for the S&P 500. By finding what he feels are the key levels for the ES, Tom can identify pivot points, profit targets and breakdown levels for the upcoming week. Over his 40+ years of trading experience, Tom has developed extensive knowledge on how the ES moves and it is his preferred market to trade. Throughout this article, we will provide a guideline on how to use some of the features of the RoadMap™ Software to map out the trading week.

To understand why Tom uses the ES to predict market direction, we must first know what the ES is and why it makes sense to use it. ES is the symbol used to identify and trade the E-mini futures contract based on the S&P 500 index. This is a highly liquid market to trade and is often considered a leading indicator of where the S&P 500 cash index (SPX) will be heading. One of the main reasons to track the ES over the SPX is the limitations set on the SPX. That is, the SPX is only open for trading during the day hours. The ES on the other hand is open for trading almost every hour of the day during the week. Starting Sunday night at 5pm CT, the ES futures will open for trading and will continue trading until 4pm CT on Monday. The only interruption is a short 15 minute break from 3:15pm CT to 3:30pm CT. This schedule is used every day of the week throughout the year, with the exception of holidays. The ES gives us more trading data and therefore more opportunity to identify key levels in which the bulls and bears try to take control.

Tom prefers to view the market from a “big picture” standpoint. To do this he watches the ES. Consider this. The S&P 500 is an index of all the biggest companies in the world. It is possibly the best indicator to determine how the economy is doing. Spending will increase during booming economic years. This will have a positive effect on company earnings, investor interest will go up and stock market prices will head higher. Now I know this is very simple reasoning, but it is true. The fact of the matter is about 75% of all stocks move with the S&P 500, and that is why Tom views the ES as the “king of the market.”

Finding Important Price Levels and Pivot Points

The Research Key found under “Tables” in RoadMap™ Software is what Tom uses to identify key price levels. First and foremost you should identify the yearly open. The market should be considered bullish for the year if the ES stays above the yearly open. The midpoint is also a key level that should be held for the current trend to continue. Next, check the quarterly, monthly, and weekly time frames and identify the same key numbers. It is important to note whether the weekly trend matches the monthly trend, the monthly trend matches the quarterly trend, and the quarterly trend matches the yearly trend. Also important to note are the High and Low numbers. In preparing for the week, Tom will reference these key levels to identify profit targets and breakdown levels. For example, if the ES opens above the previous week’s midpoint but below the high, a logical first profit target for the week may be the previous week’s high.

The Research Key will display the Open, High, Low, Close, Volume, and Midpoint for several different time frames. These include yearly, quarterly, monthly, weekly, daily, and 30 minute time frames. You can also view a custom range by right clicking and selecting “Add Range.” From here you can type in whatever time range you wish to see and then click the “Range” tab to view it.

To add symbols to the Research Key, simply right click and select “Add Symbol.” One important detail to remember when adding futures contracts is to type in the correct symbol to view a continuous contract, and these continuous contracts will vary by data feed. For example, using Schwab data we type in ES\* and not ESU21 or any other expiration. The same can be used for the Dow (YM\*) and the Nasdaq (NQ\*). We encourage you to compare the continuous contract with the current expiration contract. You will find the key numbers to be slightly different. This is because the continuous contract always accounts for the key levels based on the current month’s contract. Viewing the yearly open for ESU21 (September expiration) will give you the yearly open for what that specific contract was trading at. However, at the time of the yearly open all of the volume was on the March expiration contract, and therefore we need to reference the March contract to find the true year open.

Narrowing the Process - Finding Weekly Opportunities with DTI Stock Scanners

After you’ve identified the “big picture” of where the ES is trading, you can start to narrow down your approach and focus on the best trading opportunities for the week. Over the past few years we have added several stock scanners into the RoadMap™. Click on the Scans dropdown to find 5 Star Report, High Noon and Two Percent. Let’s focus on Two Percent and High Noon.

The Two Percent scanner looks for stocks that are at least 2% above or 2% below the current month open. The scanner also incorporates the year open, looking for stocks that are above or below. If a stock is above its yearly open and it is at least 2% above the current month open, it will filter into the Two Percent scan as a buy. If vice versa and a stock is both below the yearly open and at least 2% below the month open, it will filter into the scan as a sell.

The High Noon scanner filters a list of stock with a slightly shorter term criteria. We look at the month open and the previous week’s range. If a stock is above its month open it matches criteria one. Then it must also be above the previous week’s high, and if so it will filter into the High Noon report as a buy. On the bearish side, if a stock is below the month open and below the previous week’s low, it will filter into the report as a sell.

All of the DTI scanners also give us the Top 20 Longs and Top 20 Shorts. These are filtered simply by the top percentage gainers off the monthly open and the top percentage losers off the monthly open. Here’s an example of what the Top 20 Long results from the High Noon scanner are as we go to press with this white paper. The XLU Strategy  
Written in 2019

When investing for retirement, you should strive for consistent returns somewhere above inflation, while keeping risk to a minimum. In other words, you want CONSISTENCY and STABILITY. Your retirement account should be diversified with high quality companies, ideally dividend payers, across multiple industries. But wait! You still want to maximize your returns as much as possible, right? My goal with this white paper is to show you how you can conservatively grow your retirement account by as much as 30% every year, using a simple strategy I call the XLU Strategy. I’ll explain how the XLU Strategy works and how you can set this up every single week without having to over analyze the market. I’ll also explain why you won’t have to take on more risk than you are currently. In fact, you may even be able to lower your overall risk exposure to nothing!

What is a covered call?

If you’ve been around the option block at all then you’re sure to have heard the term covered call. If that’s the case than just skip over to the next paragraph. For those unfamiliar with covered calls, it’s relatively simple. When you sell a call option, you are obligating yourself to sell 100 shares of stock at a predetermined price. If you sell the call option without owning the underlying stock, you are selling the call naked. This is very risky, and I do not recommend it. But, if you own the stock (at least 100 shares), and then sell a call on that stock, you are selling a covered call. The term “covered” is used because you are obligating yourself to sell 100 shares of stock, but you already own the stock (it’s covered). If you need more education on stock options, please call our office at 251.652.1555.

Now for the strategy:

Like I mentioned before, stability is good when investing in a retirement account, but you always want to maximize your trading results. Selling options against the stock you own is a great way to collect extra income. And that’s the goal of the XLU Strategy; to collect income through selling covered calls every single week. To minimize risk, you should do this strategy with a stock or ETF that will not tank on you. I’ve chosen XLU, the SPDR Utilities ETF traded on the NYSE.

The XLU Strategy is a long-term method of growing your account. It can bring you returns up to 3x as much as you would normally realize by using a simple buy and hold strategy. Once you grasp the concept of what I’m about to teach you, I believe your thought process on investing for long-term growth may shift. You’ll realize how the power of options, patience and just a little bit of thinking (and not coasting – what most investors do) can help you to exponentially grow your account.

Steps to the XLU Strategy:

Step 1: On Friday of the current week, buy XLU shares. You should buy an even 100 share allotment of XLU – meaning you can buy 100 shares, 200 shares, 500 shares, etc. On the Friday I’m writing this, XLU finished trading at 60.15. Buying 100 shares of XLU would cost you $6,015 before commissions and fees.

Step 2: Once you acquire the shares go look at the XLU option chain on your trading platform. Go to next Friday’s expiration date and find the strike between 0.25-0.50 above the current share price. For example, if you bought XLU at $60.15 on Friday August 2nd, you would find the $60.50 strike call option for Friday August 9th .

Step 3: With your shares now acquired and the correct option picked out, sell the equal amount of option contracts as the number of shares you have. If you have 100 shares of XLU, you will sell 1 contract. If you own 500 shares of XLU, you will sell 5 contracts. In this example, if the $60.5 strike option will pay us $0.25, we would collect $250 if we owned 1000 shares of XLU and sold 10 call options.

Step 4: Have patience, sit back and let the week play out.  

Step 5: If XLU finishes below the strike price of the option, great! You get to keep the $250 of option premium you brought in from selling the covered call and you get to keep holding your shares. Right before the close, you will be able to buy back your call options at next to nothing and then go ahead and sell another covered call for the next Friday. If XLU finishes the week above your strike price, simply let your shares get called away. Then you can repurchase more shares and again sell the next Friday’s option.

Do not focus on the price of the option. If the price of XLU rises above your option strike, it will appear as if you are losing money on the option. That’s the incorrect way to think about it. By selling the $60.5 strike option for $0.25, you have decided you are willing to sell your shares at $60.5. If you bought XLU at $60.15 and sold at $60.5, you would net $0.35 per share of price appreciation over the week. But if you collected $0.25 per contract, you would net a total of $0.60 per contract (0.35 + 0.25 = 0.60). That is a 1% gain in just one week for basically doing nothing. No market timing needed, and that is a good deal!

What it all adds up to over one year:

If you consistently did the XLU strategy throughout the course of one year, here is a rough estimate of what I would expect our total profit to be, and we will use a 1,000-share allotment for the example.

Assumption #1: On average you will bring in $0.25 per contract every week - 1,000 x $0.25 = $250

Over the course of 52 weeks – 52 x $250 = $13,000 collected from covered call premiums 

Assumption #2: Estimate that you will be called away 10 times during the year at an average of $0.30 per shares when that occurs – 1,000 x $0.30 x 10 = $3,000 collected from capital gains when called away

Assumption #3: Historical returns for XLU are around 7.2% a year, so we will assume your 1,000 shares of XLU appreciates on average 0.14% a week (7.2% / 52 weeks). This is a bit tricky to estimate, but we will just base this appreciation off of 1000 shares of XLU at $60 initial price, appreciating roughly 0.14% a week for the 42 weeks we are not called away – 1000 x $60 x 0.14% x 42 weeks not called away = around $3,500 extra collected in capital gains per year

Total Estimated Return:

$13,000 in option premium collected from selling covered calls\+         $3,000 in capital gains when called away  
\+         $3,500 in capital gains from XLU price appreciation when not called away

\=         $19,500 profits on an average of $60,000 invested

or         32.5% return in one year of steady prices

Downside Risk:

I must also tell you that this is not a risk-free strategy, so we must prepare for the worst. What do you do if the price of XLU does not appreciate, but drops? How far would or should you let it drop before you changed your strategy? Here’s my opinion. Since the anticipated return of the XLU Covered Call Strategy over a 12-month time period of historical price trends is around 30%, I am willing to place an initial stop about 15% below entry price. This is equivalent to half of the anticipated 30% gain on the year. In our example, if we bought XLU at $60, the initial stop would go at $51 ($60 x 85% = $51), or $9 below our average cost. But remember, you can anticipate bringing in around $0.25 per contract every week you sell a covered call. If you never get called away, after 36 weeks of collecting premium ($9 / $0.25 = 36), you would essentially be risk-free, unless XLU gapped down more than 15% one morning (very unlikely).

As the old saying goes, “there is no such thing as a free lunch.” The typical utility company that makes up the XLU ETF is a historically stable investment for good reason. Utility companies provide a product that the consumer cannot live without and price that product accordingly, so they turn a profit. Having said that, there are times when even utility stock prices fall. One such occasion is when we have a major shift in long-term growth of the economy. Think dotcom bust and financial crisis. Another situation would be if interest rates suddenly started rising substantially. Utility companies are heavy borrowers and rising interest rates would hurt profitability. But as the FED just lowered interest rates, we are clearly not in an environment of fast rising interest rates, and if price appreciates steadily, like utility stocks usually do, the XLU Covered Call Strategy is one of the most lucrative passive stock market investment strategies that I know of.

Here’s the bottom line:

This is a long-term strategy using covered calls to consistently collect premiums over time. You can adjust your stop loss at any time depending on how much premium you’ve collected and if the trend in utility stocks changes. But you must put the stop in to cover yourself. Never trade without a stop, even low volatility stocks and ETFs like XLU. The ultimate goal of this strategy is to own your number of shares with zero cost. For instance, if you could collect $4 in premium without getting called away, that would essentially lower your initial $60 cost basis down to $56. Do this enough times, and your actual cost of owning the shares can eventually decrease to zero. Now that would be a sweet position to be in!

Tom Busby’s T-Square Strategy

Written in 2019

The only factor that matters when trading the markets is how you will make money. The best trades are easy - they pay you right from the start. But no matter how good your timing is, you will always have trades that work against you at some point. Therefore, you must have a developed process on how to manage bad trades. Tom created a strategy using the T-Square that he uses to manage option trades. In this white paper, we’ll cover what the T-Square is and his step-by-step process in detail.

Managing a portfolio of stocks can be difficult enough. When you add options to the mix, you can dramatically complicate things, especially when you trade option spreads. That’s why Tom came up with the T-Square concept to manage positions. The T-Square helps you develop a trade plan. It quickly identifies what positions will pay you if the market goes up, and what positions will pay you if the market goes down. Now, before we go any further, make note of this: Tom only uses his T-Square strategy if the option position he is trading has at least 10 days left until expiration.

Here’s the basics of what the T-Square looks like:

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg3eln2H0jwGMVmKYFFY8ZkCmyXtq3OPZk0lqdCnMVJjuBjxNCNeR2YHqQY6LrmRHhoYfy92S_gSSzpkAFkCJqReEeqgja83ZQneDGPZPAFwYcQBwjl87DMnCtRpbuHnT-NTBUzR8baKDHexIyO4gA6a58kD4PlbKBFb=s2048)

Now that you have a general idea of what the T-Square looks like, we need to quickly cover the DTI Method to trading the 24-hour market with our 4 Reference Bar approach.

DTI Method and 4 Reference Bar Approach

Those who understand the DTI Method and our Reference Bars can skip to the next section, but let’s take a few moments to go over the basics. The DTI Method focuses on understanding the 24-hour market, and Tom came up with a proprietary approach called Reference Bars, to help guide you through every day. All four Reference Bars are 30 minutes in length, and here are the times they occur every day.

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg3xXYBHhsUg4WzND_IUCYTbrvIURGV0cD146l2AiNhqp3JgiJmCduRO8lFMsh3xTx89wQZrMuLJokArtzgM5x3SaEJf40KuGsT87YQ6CVF7Bt8psZn_Jos8BEgAloymdK5DGseAsXIWVhshJ1y-aqvGy4-NPig=s2048)

Notice I’ve colored the text for each RB blue. Inside DTI’s RoadMap™ Market Analysis Software, Reference Bars are colored blue, easily spotted as a visual guide. Each Reference Bar represents the start of a new time segment (Asia, Europe and two U.S. segments). We will use the 30- minute range that makes up each Reference Bar to help us identify where traders will push and pull price during the day.

As a general guide to using DTI’s Reference Bars, consider the classic buy and sell signals. When you have consecutive 30-minute bar/candles in which the second bar/candle makes a higher high / higher low than the previous, you have a good signal that traders want to take price higher. The opposite is true for when you get a lower high / lower low setup. Knowing this tape reading skill, we can add in the importance of time of day with DTI’s 4 Reference Bars to increase our probabilities even further.

A few more terms to know…

A Positive Reference Bar occurs when the bar/candle directly following the RB makes a higher high / higher low. Here’s what it will look like on a chart:

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg0BBxj8nk_X0sDpbDtgVgrdJx0xAqebFQIyoub2isY_oiPE3XdDJNuABEce2lzl2KF-ac4vyU1-n9pMmMj5vcwE2C12-0-BWIv0wFp_YIR5D_odYFB_q8dEXS5Qx17T4sZqJmMl2PN4Kdv9VbDeHLH7gZrDGhl4Fw=s2048)

A Negative Reference Bar occurs when the bar/candle directly following the RB makes a lower high / lower low. Here’s what it will look like on a chart:

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg3dQ2IlYUJCIN8cInRl0yKsvPpeipr1ehe1sekIVYeu9Mzd3aU1SMGkjdPVf1ewbV4t6lOBmCMZBmc7QEf0PPcY-u3JAuZhPDnCg4jUUuRcF54TK_lebitXYHdMqprBkV_E224k6carhYJqN_PqD-i-LDHKEIR7jw=s2048)

We just covered the basics of a Reference Bar buy signal and sell signal. We use these simple patterns combined with our Reference Bar approach to enter, exit and manage trades intraday. Now let’s walk through the anatomy of a trade using Tom’s step-by-step T-Square strategy.

Reminder: The T-Square strategy is only used if you have at least 10 days left until option expiration!

Trade 1 (Step 1) – Buy a long call after RB3 (8:30 am – 9 am CT)

With a strong bull market still intact, the first step will be to enter a bullish trade. We will time the entry of a long call based off DTI’s Reference Bar approach. By analyzing the long-term and short-term trend, we can use RB3 as a guide to enter the market. If a bull trend is going to continue intraday, the probabilities of momentum continuing increases tremendously if we have a positive RB3. A positive RB3 occurs when the next 30-minute bar/candle after the reference bar makes a higher high. A positive RB3 signal will occur between 9:00 am – 9:30 am CT.

Trade 2 (Step 2) – If RB4 (12:30 pm – 1:00 pm CT) is negative, sell a higher call against the Trade 1 long call

If we catch a trend all day, we are in great shape, and may even sell the position for profit by day’s end. However, if the trend stalls, we must then be cautious of a reversal. We will use RB4 to help us recognize a potential late day reversal. This is important because we may need to hedge our long call bet overnight. The last thing you want to do when trading short-term is hold a long call overnight and have the Asian and/or European markets dump off, leaving you holding a call option that is too far underwater to stay with the trade. When buying options, a big part of making money is staying in the game.

If we are long a call option and RB4 is negative, we will sell a higher strike call than the call we hold long. A negative RB4 occurs when the next 30-minute bar/candle after the reference bar makes a lower low. A negative RB4 signal will occur between 1:00 pm – 1:30 pm CT.

Trade 3 (Step 3) and Trade 4 (Step 4) – If Tom determines his timing for Trade 1 is wrong for the immediate future, but believes the longer-term direction leading up to expiration will still pay him, he will sell a credit call spread to bring in additional premium.

Trade 3 (Step 3) – Sell the same strike call as Trade 2

Trade 4 (Step 4) – Buy a higher strike call than Trade 3

Here’s what it looks like from Tom’s T-Square perspective:

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg04_mIjx1mCYl9NQej6-txD31Y6GgMjq9n8__Y_QabiVzj8kE3LHQM4a9sqrAL2NVgliQ00NZvOG5gBlNH9hOZtNuTLyXeROfuCqoOv1mQbkTaegN-PlWc8YAdCWz5-4lgSKuCVqHArrJ_lylF9RNmFRESQ5eDseroJ=s2048)

Here’s what it looks like with an actual option trade:

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg09X1pQpQcYbpGdeVtASEKXq5TZA7jTuN_BYVOeiC5fMXw9atsS7hNiDbgJUpPc1gNM3p6aEk2Uk0ICvONfIjjMvIlHggX7nW7CnRDnjyTAioD_3Ydr_7NtjnBDCoYU231eo0WCf-6FgphRvW8i2_Dp1B56OS1nQdtN=s2048)

Remember, the T-Square approach is only used if you have at least 10 days to expiration. Trade 3 and 4 are only entered if Trade 1 immediate direction is determined to be wrong – if your timing is bad. Once Trade 3 and 4 are entered, Tom allows up to 48 hours to go by to let option premium get him back into the game. If Tom employs this strategy, his goal will eventually be to take profit on the short option legs and leave one or both long calls working, capturing profits when the market rises again.

By employing this strategy on an option position with at least 10 days until expiration, you allow options to work in your favor. We would all like to time the market perfect every time with options, leveraging our money to quick and outsized profits. However, with substantial risk involved in trading the markets, the unexpected events that might occur economically or politically, and when your timing is bad, this strategy allows you to stay in the trade until it will pay you. The T-Square approach allows you to hedge your initial directional bet in the short term, until the timing is right to go outright long again (or short if in a bear market).

Put Call Ratio  
Written in 2017

Imagine the following scenario.

Fifty investors get together to hold their annual meeting. Everything is running smoothly with each investor praising their current stock picks.

All of a sudden the mood starts to change. A news item just started sending shockwaves through the market. Maybe it was a terrorist attack, political noise, riots, poor economic news, or an uncertain FED announcement.

A few investors start to sell their holdings. Soon after a few more do the same.  

The remaining investors poke fun, accusing those sellers of having “weak hands” and claiming there to be nothing to worry about. “Besides, I have my stops in place to get me out if the market really turns down,” they say.

Then the next wave of sell orders trigger. Fear starts to spread throughout the entire room. Like dominoes, they start to sell one after another. “How far will this market fall?”

Finally nearly all 50 investors in the room have sold their holdings. The room is silent.  

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg0gMjTVDM2OhCkm2gyi1LQCNpNLUPYeGBGDUX9cZIZLEaDi1rL6vcm2fA_iFWQN1l-ztShrgSIWdKFTfuhQWa88XjY4cW4e56AfNRJpeXQVWvilAh_rbCa6zISVp7CeOYjDQO13RDzY8bv0xHCnJN2k5DVs91QDj6Q6=s2048)

The most difficult times in the market occur when investor fear is peaked. It is at this moment, the moment when there are no more sellers in the room, when the best buying opportunities occur. We all know these are the best buying opportunities, but all too often, if not always, we let these opportunities pass us by. We let fear get in the way of making educated decisions.

“The moment when there are no more sellers in the room… ”

What if I told you there was an indicator to confirm this fear in the market? This indicator will also clue you in on when a major or short-term bottom likely will occur.

I am talking about the Put/Call Ratio.  

The Put/Call Ratio is a contrarian sentiment indicator measuring the number of puts traded in comparison to the number of calls traded each day. The higher the ratio, the more bearish investors are. When the ratio moves above 1.0, investors are turning bearish and buying more puts than calls. Typically any reading around 1.10 or higher can signal a reversal point in the S&P 500. Investor’s Business Daily pinpoints any reading above 1.15 as likely reversal points in the S&P 500 or Nasdaq.

In other words, the Put/Call Ratio will give you a visual representation of the fear in the market and give you a signal to buy at a likely bottom.

Every day after the market closes, Tom will look at the Put/Call Ratio to clue him in on where the S&P 500 may be heading. On three occasions in 2017, a 1.10 reading or higher has occurred and Tom has given the buy signal.

Take a look at the following Put/Call ratio chart. The first time Tom gave the buy signal on January 18th, the reading was 1.18. By the following week, the S&P 500 had rallied 26 points and by March 1st a total of 124 points.

The second buy signal from Tom came on March 24th, when the reading was 1.10. The S&P 500 then rallied 24 points by March 30th .

On May 17th, Tom gave the buy signal when the Put/Call Ratio reached 1.10. Just ten days later the S&P 500 had rallied 73 points and peaked at nearly 100 points higher on June 19th before profit takers stepped in.

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg3Afv9oEYxG0gZqyfodymKFnJQvo6YRVZVjArB5H5-L8pdanHkfljxrypMebcyhbqxDK2XoK6es36kye7f1hI4k_SrsGnUA-3pw3dLS6LZftIVendIZ53Bym1agI08EQjGxsnoT-YF7hCJrtyMtpujH1oJjMxCdvhtJ=s2048)

Now once again Tom is giving the buy signal. The Put/Call Ratio has reached 1.18 signaling a likely short-term bottom in the S&P 500 and Tom believes the market will trend higher over the next few weeks.

Roadmap’s Seven Eleven

Now that you have an indicator to better pinpoint short-term and major bottoms, can you just blindly buy the market? If you could answer that question every time correctly you would be the best investor on the planet. The truth is you really need a method to confirm that a reversal is in order. Countless times throughout the year the S&P 500 or any other market will dip down to what looks like a short-term low and start moving up some. The most difficult and at times frustrating part is figuring out whether the rally off the bottom will hold or whether it is just normal retracement activity.

DTI’s Tom Busby and Geof Smith created the Seven Eleven feature of the Roadmap Software as a means of identifying and confirming reversals in the market. In order for the Seven Eleven to confirm a reversal the following must occur. The ES (E-mini S&P 500 futures) must hold a short-term bottom for at least an hour and then trade up at least 7 points above the bottom. Also, the NQ (E-mini Nasdaq futures) must hold a short-term bottom for at an hour and then trade up at least 11 points above the bottom. The following compass charts on the ES and NQ show a setup that confirmed a short-term reversal.

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg09trup0jt8VsSLeGuCYmXezP7JcVrhRC6ch5hNOA78o2t-yqxwwbInZGwXI7ZkDA3e-MzcLW6APY_zQHUtTWgmW7-b7XJJzZyasAE0AI1kh4bnQ8iIEzhGFYEgc9SK07xhFLbsc0X4h_mv9XzOZLdfYGUpjZ-Slg=s2048)

As you can see the ES found a bottom at 2419.50 during the 08:30am CT reference bar. This bottom was able to hold for an hour and after the 09:30 bar the ES traded up past 7 points above the bottom and continued trending higher. Below you can see the NQ also found a bottom at 5772.50 during the 08:30 reference bar. This bottom also held for an hour and after the 09:30 bar traded up past 11 points above the bottom and continued trending higher.

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg2JIgbU1-093d2Y7MOAjc0ZnmR0nI3yVTT0RjGgTM5b1jlH_qpOB4PLW4Dnmrpi_pK35y55CJizZF21cJ3dLYd4gKg1eSnsWMqXyKxTN9Lxdb10S2H2A9y658fzCV5vqWAu1CPXtbT_2Zbx1jArY0qmFLqidJePJ25f=s2048)

In order for the Seven Eleven to confirm a reversal both the ES and NQ must hold their lows and confirm the reversal. Once the confirmation occurs the Seven Eleven table will light up green as shown below. The Seven Eleven is also used to confirm a reversal to the downside. In this instance the table would light up red. You can include any of your favorite stocks or futures into the table by right clicking and selecting “Insert Row.” However you should confirm the reversal with the ES and NQ first and foremost, as the Seven Eleven resets after each reference bar.

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg2YVS1GytbkWljl8SUHb3iaS4L1-Eamgy3R9PbNs3zsVvrCnwRGo5cHeyNrG--qXy9UHiLE0CCHNWgvW0f02rkrkmrACEWvuAjYICbdA4MH5iu_LzDcapZREBzz4-g8EeFXYERfLp_964XpSKzsSCYdhsgRKIXPZI3l=s2048)

When fear is running rampant amongst investors, the ability to take a step back and view the market with an unbiased opinion is invaluable. When there are no more sellers left to sell, the probability is high for a reversal to the upside. Use the Put/Call Ratio and the Seven Eleven feature of the Roadmap Software to get on the winning side of the market. Once again Tom is getting long the market and believes we are set for a rally.

Test Yourself

Q: When the Put/Call Ratio moves above 1.0, investors have turned bearish?

A: True. A ratio above 1.0 represents investors have bought more puts than calls on that particular day.

Q: At what level should the Put/Call Ratio be before you start looking for a reversal?

A: The probability of a reversal is high when the Put/Call Ratio reaches 1.10. Any number above 1.15 signals a major reversal is likely.

Q: The Seven Eleven lights up green or red when the ES confirms a reversal?

A: False. The Seven Eleven will light up green or red when both the ES and the NQ confirm a reversal.

The 7 Sisters

Written in 2022

7 Sisters video: [https://vimeo.com/704688254](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://vimeo.com/704688254&sa=D&source=editors&ust=1777400035441019&usg=AOvVaw0QUWpU6RJp6gXhX6pAYqA8)

Tom Busby has developed the DTI Method over the years to help our students learn a repeatable process to analyze and trade the markets. A process must have a starting point. For DTI and students of the DTI Method, every trading year, month, week, day and even down to intraday time frames starts with the 7 Sisters. This white paper will first define the 7 Sisters, introduce the basics of each market and briefly describe the purpose of tracking the global markets.

7 Sisters:

ES - S&P futures

YM - Dow futures

NQ - Nasdaq futures

DAX - German index

CL - crude futures

GC - gold futures

ZB - 30 year bond futures

*   ES - E-mini S&P futures. A derivative S&P 500 cash index. The actual futures contract is trading at the CME electronically on a computer system called Globex. Trading for the week begins Sunday night at 5pm Central time and closes for the week Friday at 4pm Central. Each trading day is separated by a 1 hour halt in trading from 4pm to 5pm Central time
*   NQ - E-mini Nasdaq futures. Based on the Nasdaq 100 index and not the Composite index. On the equity this is similar to the QQQ which is based on the Nasdaq 100 and not the Composite index. Also traded at the CME on Globex and has the same trading hours as ES.
*   YM - E-mini Dow futures. Based on the Dow 30, also known as the Dow Jones Industrial Average. Also traded at the CME on Globex and has the same trading hours as ES and NQ.
*   DAX - Mini DAX futures from Germany. Just like the U.S. Dow index as it consists of 30 of the largest companies in Germany. Traded on the EUREX (European Exchange) electronically and its currency is in Euros. Trading hours are 6:15pm Central to 3pm Central the next day, opening the week’s trading Sunday night and closing out the week Friday afternoon.
*   GC - Gold futures. Traded at the CME on Globex and has the same trading hours as the ES, NQ and YM, opening the week Sunday night at 5pm Central and closing the week Friday afternoon at 4pm Central, with a one hour break in between each trading day. This is a commodity futures market.
*   CL - Crude oil futures. Also traded at the CME on Globex and also has the same trading hours as the ES, NQ, YM and GC. Like gold, the crude futures contract is a commodity futures market, and it is deliverable.
*   ZB - 30 year bond futures. Trade at the CME on Globex as well, with the same trading hours as our other Globex markets. This contract controls a $100,000 U.S. Treasury Bond.

What we are really looking for when tracking the 7 Sisters is we have the indexes, the ES, YM and NQ futures (if you would prefer to track the respective ETF’s during cash market hours that would be the SPY, DIA and QQQ), and we get to see if our indexes are moving higher or lower. If the indexes are moving lower you will approximately 70-75% of all stocks moving lower. If the indexes are moving higher you should expect around 70-75% of stocks moving higher.

When we look at the DAX we of course want to see how Europe is doing. Germany is the largest economy in Europe so we get to see how the largest economy in Europe is doing. The ETF for the DAX is not a heavily traded market so it is not a great replacement. Instead you can track a calculated price of the DAX here: https://www.investing.com/indices/germany-30-futures and the feed is very nearly a live feed.

GLD is the ETF equivalent that does a decent job tracking gold futures. Gold is a fear contract or it can be viewed as an inflation hedge as well. If gold is moving higher there is a lot of uncertainty in the market. Of course you will have currencies moving the gold market also. For example, if the U.S. Dollar is getting weak and we will see money flow into gold.

USO is our ETF for crude. The question you should ask yourself is are we having to pay higher energy prices or are energy prices lower? Energy affects everything in the U.S. and around the world so we watch energy prices.

TLT is our preferred ETF for the bond market. Bond prices are of course sensitive to interest rates, inversely. As interest rates rise, bond prices fall. As interest rates fall, bond prices rise. TLT is based off the 20 year bond instead of the 30 year bond so it is a slightly different look than the 30 year bond futures. Of course, changes in interest can have significant impacts on the health of the economy, so we must track them.

For more specifics about each of our 7 Sisters markets, please consider reviewing Geof Smith’s video introduction here: [https://vimeo.com/704688254](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://vimeo.com/704688254&sa=D&source=editors&ust=1777400035447637&usg=AOvVaw0LMyQdm16JxE1Y2V0nkP2V)

Orientation to DTI’s Reference Bars

Written in 2020

Our goal is to analyze the 24-hour market and to answer the question, long, short or out. We do so by breaking up the global trading day into four time segments: Asia, Europe, US morning and US afternoon. Reference the picture below (in Central Time).

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg23CXCg_B95LuIz-mhuVS0G6wxQLalXOz2KUkCj2MQtb_2pwYS_ixNfRAW2ABVYqAX1VvOkf79lz70X7Xv9F82IyNqYXAMVQgXUAtkwMa2kNc41VygV9TcomPdZ1yMWkHXgII8yVGX83VlVPsa0AJ6TPFRcDfsQiQ=s2048)

To keep everything in perspective, we track the 24-hour global market using our proprietary approach with DTI Reference Bars. A reference bar is simply a 30-minute candlestick or bar that appears colored in blue on our charts inside the RoadMap™ Software. On many occasions you will hear Tom mention the reference bars as “blue bars” … and in total, we use 4 blue bars to analyze and trade the 24- hour market. I’ve listed them below.

*   Reference Bar 1 (RB1), occurring from 5:00 pm CT – 5:30 pm CT is used to analyze and trade Asia’s market
*   Reference Bar 2 (RB2), occurring from 3:30 am CT – 4:00 am CT is used to analyze and trade Europe’s market
*   Reference Bar 3 (RB3), occurring from 8:30 am CT – 9:00 am CT is used to analyze and trade the U.S. morning market hours
*   Reference Bar 4 (RB4), occurring from 12:30 pm CT – 1:00 pm CT is used to analyze and trade the U.S. afternoon market hours

Simple Strategy for Short-term Trading

As another guideline for learning the DTI Method and the use of Reference Bars when trading during the day, I think it is fitting to go over the 3 main chart patterns you need to know to make money trading. These patterns generally tell you when to be long, short or stay out. Here’s a picture to display these simple but very effective chart patterns.

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg2r4avpOLQthhe3qlrxci1n_NjX8rnytADvnE_4W7MkCebI3xKm6Tc0Ra8_CXqDiYhdH6EYjqfxTgR-BosheMBn1kl7zjgZcRqI_rlFXhEHtDrcw8DqUh6ykLFtuBGEvKq3e4cylAcwj8KdfZ3toHb8C5nbfqfPXvFc=s2048)

Now let’s incorporate the Reference Bars with these 3 signals. Each Reference Bar maps out a 30-minute range. Note the high and low of that range. Using the simple patterns from above, we have a buy signal when the range of the Reference Bar is broken to the upside. If this occurs we then answer the question of where our stop goes. A simple strategy to use is if you enter a long trade off a higher high of the Reference Bar, your stop goes below the low of that same Reference Bar, as shown below

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg01Jah54KCYYUEZbTSdmBWXJ5JyZcpbAk93COFCap3TYjnLIyekhqMzk2-ys0QZievfEKr-aTeZJ8r0PP7RFL3vKsBQihMocy2dnGy8VWWcSDKJiOTcU0Qk-udPcrlUi6Lr22cwuWMIlTfo7zDumG5Ap4MrbY7R_Q=s2048)

Please do not get caught up in exact numbers. You don’t have to enter the trade one tick above the RB range; all the same as you don’t need to sell out on a stop one tick below the RB range. There is nothing exact in trading. Our goal is to catch trends intraday, and the Reference Bars set us up to capture those trends.

Let’s look at a chart for more clarity. On the chart below of the Emini S&P futures, you will see 30 minute bars, with a few of them colored blue. The blue bars are the Reference Bars. Technically, those are candlesticks and not bars, but the candlesticks are easier to see. For the DTI Method, either will work. Our Reference Bar times occur at the beginning of each Time Segment. They are 5:00pm CT, 3:30am CT, 8:30am CT and 12:30pm CT. A break in either direction of the Reference Bar range is usually an indication that traders want the take the market in that direction. If the signal reverses, and then the other side of the Reference Bar is taken out, that is a strong reversal of trend signal.

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg2BA8YUmAHvc6aXE3Zkx-z-mEZuMR-UUilD5peyOJl4wbsc1VmM2HwJA8vC5biBmgtfZZ8GZlv8rI45KQxZegQ2J1lzbn-CQU1mvf2crqcexcAN1UHNv12qqlsOiPRCMgnML1iVwSKMTLu-Fnb7nHjlMl5O-CfoaA=s2048)

You can’t tell, but the candle at the far left is a 12:30pm Reference Bar. It signifies the beginning of our 4th Time Segment during the 24-hour market. I’ve labeled it RB4, and proceeded to label all other Reference Bars on this chart. At the beginning of this chart, sellers took control during the afternoon hours of the U.S. market. Once the futures market opens up again (the first RB1), sellers remain in control and the downtrend continues. Once Europe opens (RB2), more buyers step in, reversing the trend. At the U.S. cash open at 8:30am CT (RB3), we again get a reversal, this time the selling pressure from the end of the prior day regains focus. We then have yet another reversal when traders return from lunch (RB4) and finish the trading day with a rally. A very similar pattern occurs the next day.

These reversals are unmistakable, and our placement of Reference Bar times is no coincidence. The RB4 reversal is one of the strongest trades you will find, and our proprietary Reference Bars make this crystal clear. You won’t find charts like this anywhere else but DTI’s RoadMap™ Software.

\-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Addition of RB X, Y and Z – White Reference Bars

In recent years we have added to our key Reference Bar times throughout the day. These additional times can be found below:

\-          Reference Bar X (RBX), occurring from 6:00 am CT – 6:30 am CT is used to analyze and trade the U.S. pre-market and Europe after lunch

\-          Reference Bar Y (RBY - Genesis), occurring from 10:30 am CT – 11:00 am CT is used to analyze and trade following the European close

\-          Reference Bar Z (RBZ), occurring from 2:30 pm CT – 3:00 pm CT is used to analyze and trade into the close for the U.S. market

Note that these 3 additional RBs are colored white on our RoadMap™ charts. The example below shows the S&P making a low and reversal back higher going into and after the European close RBY. Remember that each Reference Bar brings about an opportunity for the trend to accelerate or reverse. We call the RBY bar “Genesis” as it marks the first time throughout the entire trading day that the U.S. takes full control over the markets, and on many days a new trend can begin at the RBY time once any influence from Europe drops off.

![](https://docs.google.com/docs-images-rt/ABaEjg20QdzerqXuurtqReflRNK7bzt2sxq-wcj6-D-M7YuqzEAiJ1e2YQXf4pkF4NL-mTFc7q4XrqwQ2WRJZwNd5d4SEWLNMB-n0E4RJnTKyVpjo4QCpoY4CRU9GVZ0IHqgEgK_aLYyZa6X58onKtZfVdKKdzzAScyJ8x-rmg=s2048)

\-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Now let’s go a step further with a concept from Tom Busby that allows him to get a jump start on finding the trend:

“What I like to do is take the high and low (range) of the current Reference Bar (RB) and find the midpoint of that range. I then compare the currently traded price to that midpoint to determine if the immediate trend is stronger or weaker. I’ll continue to compare whatever the currently traded price is to that midpoint for the entire Time Segment, until the next Reference Bar forms, and then I’ll start the process over again.”

“As an example, if the ES opened up the cash market trading at 8:30am CT and then mapped out a 10- point range from 3350-3360 during the first 30 minutes of trading, the midpoint would be 3355. After the RB forms, as long as we are above the 3355 midpoint we are more bullish than bearish, and vice versa. “Again the midpoint of each Reference Bar is important to tell us whether we are stronger or weaker, and because we know that trends can change around the key times of day of our RB’s, we can use the midpoint to get an early indication of what kind of day (or Time Segment) we are going to have. Example: if we are bullish coming into the afternoon RB4 and the trend looks to be continuing after RB4 forms… then all of a sudden price reverses and falls below the midpoint of RB4, that’s an early indication that something is wrong.”

“Now, obviously if it breaks the low of the RB it’s an even better trade to the downside, and the same is true for the opposite scenario. This is just a real simple technique I’ve been using for years and we’re including it in the RB white paper so that everyone has a chance to understand entries and exits on different markets, and it works 24 hours around the clock. I observe it all the time in my head and it just occurred to me that I’ve never shared this knowledge in all the years I’ve been teaching.”

\-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

That’s it. Learn DTI’s Reference Bar and learn Tom’s method, the DTI Method and you will be a much more accurate trader.
